document_text:
Deep Randomized Distributed Function Computation (DeepRDFC):
Neural Distributed Channel Simulation
Didrik Bergström and Onur Günlü
Information Theory and Security Laboratory (ITSL), Linköping University, Sweden
Email: {didrik.bergstrom, onur.gunlu}@liu.se
Abstract—The randomized distributed function computation
(RDFC) framework, which unifies many cutting-edge distributed
computation and learning applications, is considered. An au-
toencoder (AE) architecture is proposed to minimize the total
variation distance between the probability distribution simulated
by the AE outputs and an unknown target distribution, using only
data samples. We illustrate significantly high RDFC performance
with communication load gains from our AEs compared to data
compression methods. Our designs establish deep learning-based
RDFC methods and aim to facilitate the use of RDFC methods,
especially when the amount of common randomness is limited
and strong function computation guarantees are required.
I. INTRODUCTION
In conventional systems, data is communicated as arbitrary
bit sequences without considering the conveyed meaning. This
deficiency has motivatedsemantic communications[1], [2],
where the quality measure of transmission depends on the
semantics [3]. For example, transmitting objects and their
relative positions, rather than raw pixel arrays, exemplifies
semantic communication [3]. As the semantics of data can be
modeled as a function of the data, the semantic communication
paradigm can be defined as a remote (hidden) source coding
problem where the transmitted information is a function of
the data, while the function output is not available to the
transmitter [4, pp. 78], [5, pp. 118], [6]. This formulation
extends beyond classical lossy coding by employing semantic-
driven fidelity metrics [7]–[9].
We consider a generalization of remote source coding that
employs arandomizedfunctional mapping for distributed
function computation. In this setting, the receiver’s compu-
tation relies on randomized transformations of the transmit-
ter’s data, thus defining arandomized distributed function
computation (RDFC)framework, introduced in [7]. RDFC
unifies a large set of important problems that leverage se-
mantic communications. Example RDFC applications include
machine learning-based data compression methods with gen-
erative models [10], [11], federated learning (FL) with side
information [12], transform coding methods [13], [14], lossy
compression methods with realism constraints (such as visual
perception) [15], [16], and compression methods used as
differential privacy mechanisms [17], [18]. These applications
aim to synthesize data according to a target joint proba-
bility distribution, which broadly corresponds to controlling
the correlation between data samples to satisfy a constraint
requiring a controlled randomization step. Minimizing thecommunication load required for RDFC is defined as acoor-
dination problem[19]–[22], also known as distributed channel
simulation/synthesis.
We focus on strong coordination [19], imposing joint
typicality constraints on each function computation. Such a
stringent constraint is vital to ensure a performance guaran-
tee for each computation instance, unlike previous methods
whose guarantee is for the average case over all computation
instances, where the latter provides empirical coordination.
Moreover, unlike computations of deterministic functions,
common randomness shared between transmitter and receiver
can significantly reduce the communication load for RDFC
[23]. Such uniformly distributed common randomness can be
obtained, e.g., by using physical unclonable functions [24]. For
instance, if encoder-decoder pairs that apply RDFC methods
are used to achieve local differential privacy, the communica-
tion load is reduced by up to 214 times compared to adding
noise for the same purpose [7]. Thus, in this work, we propose
deep learning methods to design low-complexity encoder-
decoder pairs for the RDFC framework. There are a few code
constructions proposed for empirical coordination problems,
such as in [25], designed for binary symmetric channels
(BSCs), and existential designs for strong coordination, such
as in [26]. These existing designs do not provide constructive
code designs for RDFC and do not leverage the high perfor-
mance of deep neural networks, illustrated to surpass classical
code constructions for reliable communications [27].
Our summary of the main contributions is as follows:
•We develop a general constructive design of autoencoders
(AEs) for RDFC in a discrete setting. We propose a suit-
able loss function for RDFC and algorithms to generate
training data that provide high RDFC performance.
•We provide an AE architecture and technical insights to
facilitate the training and application of AEs in RDFC.
We motivate our choices of layer activation functions,
training parameters, and the need for a vector quantizer
layer that determines the RDFC rate for our setting.
•We demonstrate the feasibility of our design by simulat-
ing a BSC in a distributed computation setting. We also
illustrate the impact of the amount of available common
randomness on the RDFC performance.

In Section II, we introduce the RDFC framework and review
AEs. In Section III, we propose AEs designed for the RDFC
framework. In Section IV, we present and discuss the results
of the experiments performed using our AE designs. Finally,
we conclude the paper in Section V.

In this point-to-point scenario, two extremes characterize the
RDFC rate region, given that there is enough local random-
ness. Without common randomness, i.e.,R 0= 0, the minimal
communication rate is equal to Wyner’s common information
(WCI)C(X;Y) = inf U:X−U−Y I(X, Y;U)[28]. With large
enough common randomnessR 0, the distribution rateRcan
be decreased toI(X;Y).
B. Review of AEs
An AE is a feed-forward neural network consisting of an
encoder that outputs an index
¯j=Enc(¯x)given an input¯x,
and a decoder that receives
¯jand reconstructs the message
b
¯y=Dec(
¯j)[29]. The AE is trained by running the above
step (forward propagation/inference) and then computing the
loss according to a loss functionL(
¯y,b
¯y). In the following
step (backpropagation), the chain rule is applied to compute
the gradients of the loss function with respect to all network
parameters, starting from the output layer and moving back-
ward through the network. These gradients are then passed
to an optimizer that updates the network parameters, thereby
reducing the loss on the next iteration.
In our setting, the AE takes the source input¯Xand
common- and local randomness¯Kand¯Las inputs, and out-
puts¯Y. The index¯Jis contained within the AE and automati-
cally adapts itself, given some training set{¯xi,
¯yi,¯ki,¯li}Ns
i=1, to
improve the RDFC performance during training. We measure
performance using the non-asymptotic TVD given in (2).
III. AE-BASEDRDFC
In this section, we first provide our proposed algorithms
to generate training data and motivate our choice of the loss
function for RDFC. Moreover, we describe our training and
testing methodology, including the vector quantization layer.
We use an AE to jointly optimize an encoder-decoder pair, as
used in [27], for reliable communications, given only samples
from the target distributionQ
¯X¯Y.

Algorithm 2 samples realizations(¯ki,¯li)uniformly ran-

via the aforementioned bins and concatenates the samples into
a training set. Furthermore, we use one-hot encoded input-

layer to interpret the output as the AE’s maximum likeli-
hood predictionb
¯ygiven(¯x,¯k,¯l). We round the output with
argmaxduring inference to emulate the AE’s intended hard
decisions. The common randomness¯Kand local randomness
¯Lare encoded as binary sequences. We implement the AE in
Keras/Tensorflow with the design given in Table I.
A. Loss Function for TVD Minimization
Using TVD as the loss function is intractable in this
setting because the gradient descent tends to get stuck in
local minima. Therefore, we use CCE as our loss function
during the training phase. CCE is a suitable surrogate loss
function to TVD due to (i) its differentiability and (ii) its
equivalence to Kullback-Liebler divergence if the AE’s inputs
and outputs are one-hot encoded, which is the case in our
design. Moreover, Kullback-Liebler divergence bounds TVD
from above via Pinsker’s inequality [30].

B. Training and Testing
As in [27], we use a large batch size (214) and a low learning
rate. We use the ADAM optimizer with parameters(lr=
10−4, β1=0.9, β 2=0.999). To adaptively lower the learning
rate when the loss plateaus, we use aReduceLROnPlateau
callback [31] with parameters(monitor=“loss", patience=1,
min_delta=0.01). We use a set of samples{¯xi,
¯yi}Ns
i=1from

C. Vector Quantizer Layer
We constrain the RDFC rateRby using a vector quantizer
(VQ) layer, as in [32]. The VQ-layer is initialized with a fixed
embedding space¯J= [
¯j1,
¯j2, ...,
¯j2nR], and we ensure that
the VQ-layer has a preceding layer with annR-dimensional
output ˜
¯j. The VQ-layer takes ˜
¯jas input and outputs the closest
discrete index as
¯ji∈¯J:i= argmin
i∥˜

Since (5) is not differentiable, the VQ-layer uses a straight-
through estimator [32] to pass the gradient from the decoder
to the layer preceding the VQ-layer during backpropagation.
We adapt the implementation in [33] to our specific design.
IV. EXPERIMENTALRESULTS
We consider a BSC(p)as our target conditional probability
distributionQ
¯Y|¯Xwith a binary uniform i.i.d. source input
sequence¯X∼Q
¯Xand a binary output sequence¯Yof block-
lengthn. Both common randomness¯Kand local randomness
¯Lare uniformly random i.i.d. binary sequences. We consider
an index¯Jthat is binary and of lengthnR=n−1, which
the VQ-layer imposes. We list the blocklengths, the other
design parameters, and the results from our experiments in
Table II. The upper half of Table II lists the experimentsTABLE II

As shown in Table II, we select the common- and local
randomness ratesR 0andR Lto be relatively large to ensure
their achievability, asymptotically defined by (3). We set
the RDFC rateRto either7/8or9/10, which suffices to
demonstrate feasibility. Moreover, we observe from Table II
that the difference between the TVD Tand the TVD Gis small
in both the LR- and LR+CR-cases, and TVD G<TVD Tfor
all experiments. We expect TVD Gto be less than TVD Tin
general, and the small differences between the two in Table II
indicate that our AE designs generalize well.
We observe that the LR-cases achieve high RDFC perfor-
mance, strictly improved by the LR+CR-cases. This improve-
ment is expected as the AE learns to leverage the common
randomness for distributed channel simulation for a fixed-rate
index¯J. Furthermore, increasingR Lalso improves the RDFC
performance, with the improvement being higher for larger
crossover probabilitiesp. This result is also expected since
the lower bound onR Lin (3) increases with increasingp.
We illustrate the similarities between joint distributions for
different cases in Fig. 2, where, e.g., the middle figure has a
TVD T≈0.34yet maintains the target structure.
In general, we observe improved RDFC performance with
both (i) lower mutual informationI(X;Y), which is≈0.5
and≈0.19forp= 0.11and0.25, respectively, and (ii)
higher RDFC rateR, which we expect by (3). However,only
increasingRleads to slightly worse performance. This is
likely due to the static sample sizeN s. We do not adapt
Nsto the different alphabet sizes between experiments due
to practical memory and time reasons, which can lead to the
training set being less representative in some experiments than

A. AE Design Insights
We observe faster convergence during training when layer
7 in Table I has the sigmoid activation function and a lower
quantization error between the encoder output ˜
¯jand the VQ
output
¯jcompared to when ReLU is used. We conjecture that
the activation function normalizes ˜
¯jto an interval close to
the latent alphabet¯J, which seems to aid in training so that
the encoder adapts better to the VQ-layer and the constraints
it imposes. Moreover, a deep encoder and decoder converge
faster than shallower architectures for a fixed network width.
Our simulations do not indicate whether this is solely due
to the larger number of network parameters or whether the
network depth alone enables more complex behavior. Finally,
in Algorithm 1, we observe increased RDFC performance
when we allowB(¯l)(¯x,
¯y)=∅for(¯x,
¯y, b)such that we have
Pr[¯Y=
¯y|¯X=¯x, B(
¯y)
b]≈0. However, allowing empty sets
inB(¯k)(¯x)shows instances of both improved and reduced
performance depending onQ
¯Y|¯Xandn.
V. CONCLUSION
We proposed AE designs for the RDFC framework, pro-
viding significant communication load gains for multiple dis-
tributed computation and learning problems. We demonstrated
that our AE designs achieve high RDFC performance for
distributed synthesis of a BSC, where the amount of common
randomness was shown to play a crucial role. Furthermore,
we provided fundamental information-theoretic insights into
the network architecture and the training-data generation.
With this work, we aimed to facilitate the use of RDFC
techniques for cutting-edge use cases, including neural image
compression, FL with side information, and distributed secure
and private function computation. Finally, while one can show
that there is a gap between the achieved rate tuples and the
asymptotic RDFC limits, such a comparison does not provide
meaningful insights due to the short blocklengths considered
in this work. Thus, in future work, we will apply, for example,
hybrid coding methods introduced in [34] to our AE designs
to consider practical blocklengths.

structure.title: Deep Randomized Distributed Function Computation (DeepRDFC): Neural Distributed Channel Simulation
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Deep Randomized Distributed Function Computation (DeepRDFC) and its application in neural distributed channel simulation. The main points covered include the introduction of the RDFC framework, which unifies various distributed computation and learning applications, and the proposal of an autoencoder (AE) architecture to minimize the total variation distance between the probability distribution simulated by the AE outputs and an unknown target distribution. The document also highlights the importance of semantic communications, where the quality measure of transmission depends on the semantics, and the use of randomized functional mapping for distributed function computation. Additionally, it touches on the concept of strong coordination, which imposes joint typicality constraints on each function computation, and the role of common randomness in reducing the communication load for RDFC. The authors propose deep learning methods to design low-complexity encoder-decoder pairs for the RDFC framework and provide a summary of their main contributions, including the development of a general constructive design of autoencoders for RDFC and the proposal of a suitable loss function and algorithms for generating training data.

nodes.title: I. INTRODUCTION
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Deep Randomized Distributed Function Computation (DeepRDFC) and its application in neural distributed channel simulation. The main points covered include the introduction of the RDFC framework, which unifies many cutting-edge distributed computation and learning applications, and the proposal of an autoencoder (AE) architecture to minimize the total variation distance between the probability distribution simulated by the AE outputs and an unknown target distribution. The document also reviews the system model and existing AE designs, and proposes a new AE-based RDFC method that leverages deep learning to design low-complexity encoder-decoder pairs for the RDFC framework. The key contributions of the work include the development of a general constructive design of AEs for RDFC, the proposal of a suitable loss function for RDFC, and the demonstration of the feasibility of the design through simulations. The document also discusses the impact of common randomness on the RDFC performance and provides a mathematical formulation of the RDFC rate region for the distributed channel synthesis problem. Overall, the partial document presents a novel approach to RDFC using deep learning and AE architectures, with potential applications in semantic communications, machine learning, and data compression.

nodes.title: II. SYSTEM MODEL AND REVIEW OF AES
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Distributed Channel Simulation and the Rate-Distortion Framework with Common Randomness (RDFC). It introduces the system model, reviews Autoencoders (AEs), and proposes AE designs for the RDFC framework. The main points covered include the definition of the RDFC rate region, the distributed channel simulation problem, and the use of AEs to jointly optimize an encoder-decoder pair for reliable communications. The document also defines various mathematical concepts such as total variation distance, Markov chains, and loss functions like categorical cross-entropy. Additionally, it outlines the structure of the document, including the introduction of the RDFC framework, proposal of AE designs, presentation of experimental results, and conclusion.

nodes.title: A. Distributed Channel Simulation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Distributed Channel Simulation and the Rate-Distortion Framework with Common Randomness (RDFC). It introduces the system model, reviews Autoencoders (AEs), and proposes AE designs for the RDFC framework. The main points covered include the definition of the RDFC rate region, the distributed channel simulation problem, and the use of AEs to jointly optimize an encoder-decoder pair for reliable communications. The document also defines various mathematical concepts such as total variation distance, Markov chains, and categorical cross-entropy loss function. Additionally, it outlines the structure of the document, including the introduction of the RDFC framework, proposal of AE designs, presentation of experimental results, and conclusion.

nodes.title: B. Review of AEs
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the concept of Randomness-Constrained Distribution Formation Coding (RDFC) and its application to distributed channel simulation. The main points covered include the introduction of the RDFC framework, review of Autoencoders (AEs), and the proposal of AE-based RDFC designs. The document also defines the RDFC rate region and provides a theorem that characterizes this region. Additionally, it discusses the training and testing methodology for AE-based RDFC, including the use of binning algorithms and loss functions such as categorical cross-entropy (CCE). The overall goal is to synthesize a joint distribution between the input and output of a channel, given certain constraints on the available randomness. The document also provides algorithms for constructing training data and implementing the AE design.

nodes.title: III. AE-BASED RDFC
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the implementation of an Autoencoder (AE) for a specific task, focusing on the construction of a training set and the definition of the AE's architecture. The main points covered include the notation and definition of the AE, the dependence of its performance on the distribution of samples in the training set, and the introduction of two algorithms: Algorithm 1 for binning of variables K and L, and Algorithm 2 for generating training data. The document also describes the architecture of the AE, including the use of one-hot encoded input-output vectors, softmax activation function, and the implementation in Keras/Tensorflow. Additionally, it discusses the choice of loss function, opting for Cross-Entropy (CCE) as a surrogate to Total Variation Distance (TVD) due to its differentiability and equivalence to Kullback-Liebler divergence.

nodes.title: A. Loss Function for TVD Minimization
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the implementation of an Autoencoder (AE) for a specific task, focusing on the construction of a training set and the definition of the AE's architecture. The main points covered include the notation and definition of the AE, the dependence of its performance on the distribution of samples in the training set, and the introduction of two algorithms: Algorithm 1 for binning of variables K and L, and Algorithm 2 for generating training data. The document also describes the implementation details of the AE, including the use of one-hot encoded input-output vectors, the softmax activation function, and the encoding of common and local randomness as binary sequences. Additionally, it discusses the choice of loss function, opting for Cross-Entropy (CCE) as a surrogate to Total Variation Distance (TVD) due to its differentiability and equivalence to Kullback-Liebler divergence.

nodes.title: B. Training and Testing
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the implementation of an Autoencoder (AE) for a specific task, focusing on the construction of a training set and the definition of the AE's architecture. The main points covered include the notation and definition of the AE, the dependence of its performance on the distribution of samples in the training set, and the introduction of two algorithms: Algorithm 1 for binning of variables K and L, and Algorithm 2 for generating training data. The document also describes the implementation details of the AE, including the use of one-hot encoded input-output vectors, the softmax activation function, and the encoding of common and local randomness as binary sequences. Additionally, it discusses the choice of loss function, opting for Cross-Entropy (CCE) as a surrogate for Total Variation Distance (TVD) minimization due to its differentiability and equivalence to Kullback-Liebler divergence.

nodes.title: C. Vector Quantizer Layer
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the implementation of an Autoencoder (AE) for distributed random functional computation (RDFC) with common and local randomness. The main points covered include the construction of a training set using Algorithms 1 and 2, the design of the AE architecture, and the use of a vector quantizer layer to constrain the RDFC rate. The document also presents experimental results for different cases, including those with only local randomness (LR-cases) and those with both local and common randomness (LR+CR-cases). The results show that the AE designs generalize well, with small differences between the test TVD and ground truth TVD, and that the LR+CR-cases achieve better RDFC performance than the LR-cases. Additionally, the results demonstrate that increasing the local randomness rate improves the RDFC performance, especially for larger crossover probabilities.

nodes.title: IV. EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the training and testing of an Autoencoder (AE) for a Rate-Distortion-Perception (RDP) framework, specifically for distributed synthesis of a Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC). The AE is trained using a large batch size and a low learning rate, with an ADAM optimizer and a ReduceLROnPlateau callback to adaptively lower the learning rate. The document also describes the use of a Vector Quantizer (VQ) layer to constrain the RDFC rate. Experimental results are presented, showing the performance of the AE in terms of Total Variation Distance (TVD) between the estimated and target joint distributions. The results indicate that the AE achieves high RDFC performance, with improved performance when common randomness is available to both the encoder and decoder. The document also discusses the design insights and limitations of the AE, and provides conclusions and future work directions.

nodes.title: A. AE Design Insights
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the design of Autoencoders (AE) for Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC) framework, which provides significant communication load gains for distributed computation and learning problems. The document presents experimental results, including sample sizes and ratios of combinations, and provides insights into the network architecture and training-data generation. It also explores the impact of activation functions, quantization error, and network depth on the convergence of the encoder and decoder. The document concludes by highlighting the potential applications of RDFC techniques in cutting-edge use cases, such as neural image compression and distributed secure function computation. Additionally, it references various research papers and acknowledges funding support from several organizations.

nodes.title: V. CONCLUSION
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the design of Autoencoders (AE) for Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC) framework, which provides significant communication load gains for distributed computation and learning problems. The document presents experimental results, including sample sizes and ratios of combinations, and provides insights into the network architecture and training data generation. It also discusses the impact of activation functions, quantization error, and network depth on the convergence of the AE. The document concludes by highlighting the potential applications of RDFC techniques, including neural image compression, federated learning, and distributed secure function computation. Additionally, it references various related works and research papers in the field of information theory, machine learning, and communication systems.

nodes.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENT
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the design of Autoencoders (AE) for Randomized Distributed Function Computation (RDFC) frameworks, which aim to reduce communication loads in distributed computation and learning problems. The document presents experimental results and insights into the performance of AE designs, including the impact of activation functions, network depth, and quantization error on convergence and performance. It also explores the role of common randomness in achieving high RDFC performance and provides fundamental information-theoretic insights into the network architecture and training-data generation. The document concludes by highlighting the potential applications of RDFC techniques in cutting-edge use cases, such as neural image compression, federated learning with side information, and distributed secure and private function computation.

nodes.title: REFERENCES
nodes.summary: The partial document appears to be a list of references related to information theory, machine learning, and data compression. The main points covered include lossy compression, rate-distortion-perception tradeoff, locally differentially private mechanisms, federated learning, distributed channel synthesis, quantum channel capacity, entanglement-assisted capacity, communicating probability distributions, communication complexity of correlation, multiple access channel simulation, physical unclonable functions, channel simulation with polar codes, empirical and strong coordination, deep learning for the physical layer, common information of dependent random variables, dimensionality reduction with neural networks, network information theory, and neural discrete representation learning. The references also touch on topics such as vector-quantized variational autoencoders, concatenated classic and neural codes, and low-complexity transforms. Overall, the partial document seems to be focused on the intersection of information theory and machine learning, with a emphasis on compression, communication, and learning.
